i.From the Principal

To the leaders of the TRUTH Team,

For nineteen years, Newark Collegiate has been a school. This year, our twentieth, I am asking us to become something we have always quietly been: a collegiate community of leaders, where every part of the building has a person who is proud to lead it.

When I renamed our departments as Schools and Offices, it was not for the sound of it. Words shape what we believe is possible. A department waits to be told. A School leads itself. An Office holds a charge. The names are a promise about how we will work together, and about what I expect each of you to own.

Here is the heart of it. I do not believe one person can reach every room of a house this size and keep it warm. I believe in distributive leadership: I lead through you, and you lead through the people you coach. This house runs on one vision. It is mine to stamp, and all of ours to uphold. You take that vision and you drive it into your departments, your rooms, your people. The vision is mine to set. The leading is yours to do, and it is real leading. You assist and direct the vision, and that is no small charge. Every person on this team has a line to me, solid for some and dotted for others, and I take both with equal weight. A dotted line is not a lesser line. It is my promise that I am still responsible for you, your growth, and your success. That is why I ask you to see yourselves as mini-principals. It is the truth of the work: you own the results of your room.

This house stands on two structures. The Schools and the House System run vertically, top to bottom through all four years. Our grade levels run horizontally, across one cohort at a time. Where those two lines cross, a student is fully held: known for what they learn and known for who they are. That is how we live out Rooted in Excellence, Built on Belonging.

None of it works without our values. Be the Change is not a poster in this house. It is the way we coach, the way we decide, and the way we treat one another when no one is watching. Lead your room with Kid Focus, Radical Love, Excellence, Team, and Fun, and the rest of this handbook will take care of itself.

Read this as an invitation, not an instruction. Then make your room your own.

With pride and high expectations,

Sharmaine Lewis

Principal, Newark Collegiate

ii.Be the Change · KIPP NJ

The values that live in every room.

Five values hold this house together. They belong to all of us, in every School, every Office, and every Championship. We lead as one Team first, and hold ourselves to Excellence second.

Kid Focus

Promises to kids are sacred. We lead with our students at the center, always.

Radical Love

We choose love, especially when it is hard. We see the best in people and call it forward.

Excellence

Good is not the goal. We hold a high bar, and we help each other clear it.

Team

We are stronger together. We share the load, the credit, and the hard truths.

Fun

Joy is fuel. A school worth attending is a school worth working in.

Where the values live in this house

Kid FocusOur Topline Goals, GPA and SAT, and the charge of every Room. Promises to kids drive what each room owns.
Radical LoveThe TRUTH Team Beliefs and the House System, where Championships refuse to give up on a single kid.
ExcellenceThe Topline Goals and the way every Assistant Principal owns the results of their room and holds a high bar.
TeamHow We Lead and the Beliefs: one first team, publicly aligned, leading through the people we coach.
FunThe School of Enrichment and your Team Culture Plan, where joy is planned on purpose, month by month.
iii.How We LeadTeam

Leadership that travels.

Distributive leadership is not a softer way to lead. It is a stronger one. The wisdom of this building does not live in one office. It lives in every room, carried by the person who leads it.

I lead through you. You lead through the people you coach. They lead through their students. That is how a house this size stays warm: not by one person reaching every room, but by every room having someone who leads it.

The Principal#TheSchoolLeaderHolds the vision for the whole house and coaches the mini-principals who lead it.
The Assistant Principals#TheAssistantPrincipalsAsked to see themselves as mini-principals: APs who lead a School or Office as their own, alongside their DCs and the grade-level APCs. They coach, they own results, and they keep a living culture plan.
The TeamsDCs, GLCs, Deans, APIs, social workers, teachers, and staff who do the daily work, coached and trusted to lead their lanes.
The StudentsThe reason for every room. The standard against which every decision is measured.
iv.TRUTH Team · What I BelieveRadical LoveTeam

Team Beliefs.

▲ KIPP Newark · Newark Collegiate

This is what I believe about you, and the standard I hold myself to first. How you show up matters, because you are all in for Newark Collegiate. You lead as part of a first team, and your unity and alignment drive the success of this school.

  1. You put your students first in every decision, because promises to kids are sacred, and you take full ownership of the results and progress of Newark Collegiate, not just the teams you lead.Kid FocusExcellence
  2. Your first team is the TRUTH Team. It is the team you belong to before any other, and it comes before the teams you lead. With the people you lead, you are not their peer, you are their leader: you set the vision, you make the call, and you own the decision.Team
  3. You drive results through others, coaching and supporting the people you lead and holding them, and one another, accountable.Excellence
  4. You lead with presence and authenticity, bringing steady energy while naming stress, joy, or vulnerability when the moment calls for it.Team
  5. You challenge each other directly, facing the dragon, naming hard truths, giving real feedback, and stepping into discomfort for the sake of growth.Radical Love
  6. You present as a united front, publicly aligned and privately honest, and you show up for each other.Team
  7. You walk in the shoes of others, leading with ownership, grace, and care before judgment or offense. It is not personal.Team
  8. You bring your experience, expertise, and resources to the table in service of our shared vision.Team
  9. You hold space for both excellence and vulnerability, sharing wins, anxieties, and requests for help.Team
  10. You affirm the worth and limitless potential of every student and teammate, you celebrate the identity and culture in this building, and you challenge anything that does not match our values.Radical Love
  11. You are present and intentional with your time, limiting interruptions and naming multi-tasking when needed.Excellence
  12. You communicate your comings and goings proactively, because transparency builds trust and alignment.Team
  13. You take the work seriously, not yourself, celebrating wins big and small and bringing energy, gratitude, and joy to a serious mission.Fun

Real Love. Panthertown.

The Compact · from me to you

What you can expect from me.

You read what I believe about you. A team built on trust runs both ways, so here is what you can hold me to, in writing.

I am part of your room.

I will know the work and each of your people the way you do, and you will see me in it.

I will tell you the truth, kindly and early.

I will name what I see while there is still time to use it, and I will say it to your face before I say it anywhere else.

I will help you protect your time.

I will help you build a clear arc to your week, so your hours go to what your role most needs and nothing crowds out what matters.

I will hold the hard week with you.

When a week tests every system in this house, you will not carry it alone, and I will make the call when the call is mine to make.

I will get out of your way.

Once we are clear on the vision and the decision is yours, I will trust you to lead it, and I will say so out loud.

With you, Sharmaine

One Pride, Built on Trust

How we talk.

A first team lives or dies on how it communicates. Here is how we do it.

We say the thing directly, with care.

If it matters, it gets said to the person, not around them. We give frank input while a decision is still open, and once the call is made, we rally behind it as one.

We face the dragon in the room, not the hallway.

We challenge each other to our faces, name the hard thing early, and then we repair. Disagreement is not disloyalty. Talking behind each other is.

We are clear on who decides.

Most things are a conversation. Some are a vote. Some are mine to call, and I will tell you plainly which is which.

We communicate our comings and goings.

You tell each other, and you tell me, where you are and what you are carrying.

What is said in our room stays in our room.

A first team that leaks cannot be a first team.

And here is how you keep me close enough to have your back.
Copy me on feedback.

When you send coaching or evaluative feedback to someone you lead, copy me, so I am never surprised and can back you when it counts.

Put me on the milestone moments.

Include me on the agendas for full grade-level milestones, quarterly awards, the celebrations that matter.

Invite me to the human things.

Birthdays, anniversaries, the staff moments worth marking. I would rather be told about ten than miss the one that mattered.

When I am in the loop, I can advocate, protect, and celebrate. When I am surprised, I can only react. Keep me close, and I can do my part of the Compact.

v.Topline Goals · SY27Kid FocusExcellence

Two numbers tell the story.

Everything in this house points at two outcomes. They are how we will know, together, that the year worked.

Topline Goal One
GPA

Every student on track, every quarter. GPA is how we see whether learning is sticking, and it is the first thing we will not look away from. Do the work, see the work.

Topline Goal Two
SAT

The score that opens doors. We prepare every student to walk into test day ready, because All Means All includes the test that follows them to college.

vi.The Frame of the House

Two structures, holding one student.

Every student at Collegiate is held by two structures at once. One runs up and down through all four years. The other runs across a single grade. Together they make sure no student falls through.

Runs through all four years

Vertical structures: Schools & Offices

These carry content, talent, and belonging from ninth grade to graduation.

  • The three Schools. STEM, HUM, and Enrichment, each led by Assistant Principals and Department Chairs.
  • The House System. Every student belongs to a Championship, our name for advisory, with about four Championships per grade level.
Runs across one grade

Horizontal structures: Grade Levels

These carry the daily experience of one cohort of students.

  • The grade levels. Lower School (9 and 10) and Upper School (11 and 12), each band led by an APC.
  • The QUAD team. Each APC leads a QUAD: a GLC, a Dean, an API, and a Social Worker. The GLC sets the grade's vision and its school-aligned theme.

House System   Championships run vertically through every grade · about four per grade level
Each grade's QUAD   GLC · Dean · API · Social Worker, led by the APC

Every dot is a student known twice over: by a School that teaches them and a grade-level team that watches over them. The Championships make sure that every kid, all four years, has an adult who refuses to give up on them.

vii.The Map of the House

Every room under one roof.

Three Schools carry the academic life of Collegiate. Five Offices carry everything that makes that learning possible. Every room has a leader who runs it like a mini-principal.

A School teaches. It owns instruction, talent, and student learning in its content.
An Office enables. It owns a system the whole school depends on.
The House System belongs. Its Championships run vertically through the Schools and across the grades.
viii.Who Leads What

A shared language for leadership.

So everyone knows what every title means.

Mini-PrincipalA mindset
How we ask every AP to see themselves: as the principal of their own School or Office, owning its success and its results.
APAssistant Principal
Leads a School or Office as a mini-principal. In the Schools, partners with a DC over a content area.
DCDepartment Chair
Partners with the AP to lead content, curriculum, and teacher practice. In the Office of SPED, this role is the Learning Specialist Coordinator.
Big RockWhole-school contribution
The schoolwide initiative an AP owns beyond their room, their contribution to the whole house.
APCAssistant Principal of Culture
Leads a grade band and its QUAD team, owning the daily experience of those cohorts.
QUADThe grade-level team
The four people who surround a grade together: GLC, Dean, API, and Social Worker, led by the APC.
GLCGrade Level Chair
Sets the vision and the school-aligned theme for the grade, and coordinates its teachers.
DeanGrade Level Dean
Holds the grade's culture, belonging, and behavior systems.
APIAssistant Principal of Instruction
Coaches instruction across the grade, within the QUAD. Often a School AP serving on a grade team.
Social WorkerStudent support
Protects the wellbeing of every student in the grade.
ChampionHouse System
Leads a Championship, our name for advisory, and refuses to give up on a single kid.

The Grade Levels · horizontal

For GLCs: how to think about your grade theme

As the GLC, you set the vision for your grade and name a school-aligned theme that grounds the year. A strong theme is more than a slogan. It gives a class a name, an identity to live up to, and a few words they can return to when the year gets hard.

Use the Gladiator Team as your model. It began when I led a grade as a new assistant principal, with an identity I borrowed from the world of Scandal. It had a name, a creed that captured the mindset, gladiators don't run; they put on their suits and get to work, and a grounding recitation, the Excuses piece, that reminded the team excuses accomplish nothing. That standout year set a tone that has carried through Collegiate ever since.

Build yours the same way: a name, a source of identity, a creed or rallying line, and a text that grounds it.

x.The Mini-Principal MindsetExcellence

What it means to lead a room.

Mini-principal is not your title, your title is Assistant Principal. It is how I am asking you to see yourself: as the principal of your own piece of Newark Collegiate. Three things are yours to own, and no one will own them for you.

first

The Vision

You set the direction for your room and you say it out loud often enough that your team can finish the sentence. If the vision is fuzzy, that is your work, not theirs.

second

The People

You lead through the people you coach. Their growth is your scoreboard. You see their work, name it honestly, and build them toward leading rooms of their own.

third

The Big Rock

Beyond your room, you carry one whole-school contribution, your Big Rock. It is how your leadership reaches the whole house, not just your lane.

And the part I will not soften

Each of you is responsible for the success and the results of your School or Office. This year that means GPA and SAT. We do the work, and we see the work. The numbers belong to the person who leads the room.

Reporting Lines

Who manages whom, and what I still hold.

Every one of you has a line to me. Here is what the lines mean, and who holds them.

Solid line manager

Your direct manager. They own your evaluation, your development, and your day to day leadership.

Dotted line manager

A partner manager who guides and supports your work in a specific area, without being your primary evaluator.

No matter who manages you, every one of you is a key member of the School Leadership Team. I lead this team, and I count on you to keep me current on the progress, the decisions, and the things that happen in your School or Office.

xii.The Team Culture PlanFun

Plan your team's culture, month by month.

Every Assistant Principal is required to build a Team Culture Plan. For each month of the year, name what you will do for your small team that will positively impact its culture. Draft it before August 10, bring it to our summer sessions, and keep it living all year.

<

Twenty Years

Twenty years, and I have been here for sixteen.

Same promise. A bigger room.

I came in the school's fourth year, the first year it was a fully formed school, a newish teacher who had no idea she would one day be the longest-standing principal this place has had.

We started in a conference room. Six hundred students, and graduations held in the ballroom of a local hotel. Look where we hold them now. A field. Then NJIT. Then NJPAC. And now a historic hall, a stage built for the kind of arrival we always believed our kids deserved.

The room got bigger because the work did. We are eight hundred students now. We teach AP and dual enrollment, and our graduates leave here for the Ivy League. Our own teachers are good enough that the College Board flies them out to grade the exams our students sit for. Kids leave campus for paid internships. We have an athletics program that wins. And through a pandemic that scattered schools across this country, half of this staff stayed.

I pushed, for years, that every School here would host real programs of its own, and that push gave us the STEMspy, the spelling bees, the history debates. Not extras. Proof that a School is a place that makes things happen.

Some of what I am proudest of you will never see from a stage. Advocating until our school support team was paid more like the professionals they are. Reworking how we evaluate teachers. Building leadership documents and rubrics here that were carried up to our region, and then to our foundation. The work did not stay in this building. It traveled.

And here is the one that still moves me most. Families have sent us their children, and then their younger ones, and then the ones after that. People who know us best keep choosing us. There is no higher vote than that.

None of it came easy, and not all of it came with applause. You are not joining a school that has arrived. You are joining one that keeps arriving, and the next chapter is now in this room, in your hands.

In closing

Nevertheless.

There will be a week this year that tests every system in this handbook. There always is.

Nevertheless, we will have built a house with enough leaders in it that no single hard day can bring it down. That is the whole reason for the rooms, the structures, the Championships, the plans, and the trust between us.

Lead your room. I will hold the house.

Let's get to work.

Before we begin

Before we begin, I want to know you.

This year I am leading ten leaders, and I lead each of you differently, on purpose. Tell me how to lead you well: what you need from me, what brings out your best, and what I should know about you. And while you are there, tell me what landed from this house. Then we begin.

Tell me about yourselfOpen the survey ↗
The House · Newark Collegiate
Rooted in Excellence, Built on Belonging · SY27 · Year 20
